predictions.predicted_answer: To optimize the API response time and ensure it's under 300ms for a smooth user experience on 4G mobile networks, I'd like to implement a caching mechanism for the OpenWeather API responses. However, considering the 1,000 daily call limit, we need to balance between making enough requests to update the cache regularly and not exceeding the quota. Here's an example of how we could implement a simple caching layer using JavaScript, taking into account the need to keep the app size under 2.5MB and preferring lightweight solutions: ```javascript class WeatherCache { constructor(ttl = 300000) { // 5 minutes TTL by default this.cache = {}; this.ttl = ttl; } get(city) { if (this.cache[city] && this.cache[city].expires > Date.now()) { return this.cache[city].data; } return null; } set(city, data) { this.cache[city] = { data, expires: Date.now() + this.ttl }; } } const cache = new WeatherCache(); async function fetchWeather(city) { const cachedResponse = cache.get(city); if (cachedResponse) { return cachedResponse; } try { const response = await fetch(`https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather?q=${city}&units=metric&appid=${process.env.OPENWEATHER_API_KEY}`); if (!response.ok) { throw new Error(`HTTP error! status: ${response.status}`); } const jsonData = await response.json(); cache.set(city, jsonData); return jsonData; } catch (error) { console.error('Error fetching weather:', error); return null; } } // Example usage fetchWeather('London').then(data => console.log(data)).catch(error => console.error(error)); ``` This example uses a simple in-memory cache, which might not be suitable for production environments where you might want to use a more robust caching solution like Redis or an external caching service. However, for the sake of keeping the app lightweight and under 2.5MB, this approach could be a starting point. Given the constraints and the goal to keep the API response time under 300ms, we should also consider optimizing the network requests, possibly by using a CDN for static assets or leveraging HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 features if supported by the target browsers and servers. ->-> 1,23
expected_answers: 1,200 calls per day

Here's a sample checklist I've started: ```html <!-- Checklist for semantic HTML and accessibility compliance --> <!-- Header tagging --> * Is the <header> element used correctly? * Are all headings (h1-h6) used in a logical order? <!-- Sectioning elements --> * Are <section>, <article>, <aside>, and <nav> elements used to define page sections? * Is the <main> element used to wrap the main content of the page? <!-- Image accessibility --> * Do all images have alt text? * Is the alt text descriptive and accurate? <!-- Other accessibility features --> * Are all interactive elements (buttons, links, etc.) accessible via keyboard navigation? * Is the page's content readable and understandable when JavaScript is disabled? ``` I'd like to add more items to this checklist to ensure I'm covering all aspects of semantic HTML and accessibility compliance.
